Name:  ___.              Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   F
 
Service: NEUROLOGY
 
Allergies: 
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions
 
Attending: ___
 
Chief Complaint:
Weakness
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None
 
History of Present Illness:
Ms. ___ is a ___ F w PMHx of recent Alzheimer's
diagnosis who presents to ___ ED after being referred in by 
her
cognitive neurologist after examination was concerning for signs
and symptoms of ALS.
Per report, Ms. ___ has been experiencing a progressive
dysarthria, dysphagia, and fractured sleep for the last ___
months. Prior to that, there were some cognitive concerns and a
recent diagnosis of Alzheimer's dementia in ___. Since
then she has been living with her daughter - who is present at
the bedside to report the following history.
Ms. ___ current troubles began with shortness of breath
and trouble lying flat to sleep ~3 months ago. She complained of
pain in her chest and difficulty breathing when flat. Donepezil,
which had been started for AD, was entertained as a cause and
this was discontinued with no improvement in symptoms. Ms.
___ became progressively sleepier and sleepier during the
days, exacerbating her already present cognitive difficulties. 
In the last 2 months, she began to developed slurred speech and
dysphagia. At first her trouble was with thin liquids 
(aspiration
shown on a Barium swallow study) which has now progressed to
difficulty swallowing solids as well. She has been evaluated by
ENT and Pulmonology for her swallowing and respiratory concerns,
without definitive diagnosis. 
Her daughter also reports progressive weakness, more pronounced
in the upper extremities. Ms. ___ has difficulty with her
hands - opening jars and buttoning clothes. Her daughter also
reports that the family noticed fasciculations in the BUEs and
neck last ___ when Ms. ___ was wearing short sleeve
shirts.
Ms. ___ was evaluated in cognitive neurology clinic by Dr.
___ on the day of presentation. He was concerning by her
progressive picture, which seems most consistent with ALS.

 
Past Medical History:
- recent diagnosis of Alzheimer's type dementia
- recent sleep study: no REM sleep
- recent barium swallow: aspiration with thin liquids
- overactive bladder
 
Social History:
___
Family History:
NC
 
Physical Exam:
ADMISSION EXAM:
VS T97.2 HR85 BP125/73 RR28 Sat95RA
GEN - elderly woman, head drooped, slurred speech
HEENT - NC/AT, MMM, no ptosis
NECK - weakness of neck flexors 4+/5, extensors strong
CV - RRR
RESP - moderately increased WOB with accessory muscle use, no
retractions, mild tachypnea to RR max of 31
ABD - soft, NT, ND
EXTR - atraumatic, WWP

NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION:
MS - Awakes to voice, but will fall asleep without verbal
stimulation. Oriented to self and date. Knows she is in a
hospital but cannot recall the name. Speech is extremely slurred
and difficult to understand, but there do not appear to be
language deficits. Further MS testing is compromised by
difficulty understanding speech. Voice is hypophonic and
enunciation of guttural sounds is quite compromised. No evidence
of apraxia or neglect.

CN - VFF to confrontation. PERRL. EOMI. No ptosis. No reported
diplopia. Facial sensation intact to LT. Face is symmetric at
rest and with activation - though there does appear to be
bilateral bulbar muscle weakness. Hearing intact to voice. 
Palate
with minimal excursion. Hypophonia with relatively more
compromised guttural sounds (haha, gaga). SCMs and traps full
power. Tongue with global atrophy.

MOTOR - Normal bulk. +Fasciculations in the BUEs (delts) and 
neck
muscles. BUEs with deltoid (___), tricep (4+/5) weakness. L ECR
and FEx are 4+/5. BLEs appear grossly full strength, with the
exception of 4+/5 at bilateral IPs.

SENSORY - Grossly intact to LT and temperature throughout.

REFLEXES - Markedly hyperreflexive throughout. 3+ at BUEs and
patellars. +Pec jerks bilaterally. Toes are down going
bilaterally.

COORD - No gross evidence of truncal or appendicular ataxia.

GAIT - Rises unassisted, steadier than expected. Able to take 
few
steps unaided.

 
Pertinent Results:
___ CT Head
1. No acute intracranial process. 
2. Moderate to severe age related involutional changes and 
moderate small 
vessel ischemic changes. 

___ CXR
Low lung volumes accentuate the bronchovascular markings.  
Elevated left 
hemidiaphragm with gas distended stomach and bowel beneath.  
Bibasilar 
atelectasis. 

___ EMG
Abnormal study. Together with the clinical presentation, the 
electrophysiologic findings are most consistent with a 
generalized disorder of motor neurons or their axons, as can be 
seen in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. However, chronic and 
ongoing left cervical and lumbosacral polyradiculopathies cannot 
be entirely excluded. 
There is incidental evidence for a median neuropathy at the left 
wrist, as in carpal tunnel syndrome. 
Results of repetitive nerve stimulation and short exercise 
testing point away from a disorder of neuromuscular transmission 
such as myasthenia ___.  
 
Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted to the neurology service for workup of 
neuromuscular disorder as above.

She was admitted to the stepdown unit, however in the morning 
appeared sleepy, with head drop, tachypnea and ABG showing 
hypercarbia to the ___ and hypoxia to the ___. She was 
transferred to the ICU for BiPAP.

ICU COURSE:
No EMG was available on the weekend, and so while in the ICU, 
tensilon test was performed to look for signs of myasthenia 
___. This was positive - neck flexion improved as did eye 
closure strength, although speech remained hypophonic. She was 
then started on mestinon and was uptitrated. A pheresis line was 
placed and the patient received 2 sessions of PLEX. She was 
intermittently on and off BiPAP, with repeated ABGs that showed 
continued hypoxia and hypercarbia. She started developing skin 
breakdown on her nose from the BiPAP mask, which was treated 
with wound care.

EMG was performed (see results section), which established a 
diagnosis of ALS. The tensilon test can be positive in the 
setting of ALS and certain other neurological disorders. 
Mestinon and plasma exchange were discontinued. After discussion 
with the family, the patient was made DNR/DNI.

The patient was acutely worse on the morning of ___, with 
somnolence that did not improve with BiPAP. After further 
discussion with the family and palliative care consult, the 
decision was made to focus on comfort care. She was transferred 
out of the ICU.

In reviewing her outside MRI, her diagnosis of dementia is more 
likely frontotemporal dementia, which can be associated with 
ALS, rather than Alzheimer's dementia.

___ passed away on ___ at 0143AM. She had absent
pupillary responses, no pulses, and she did not have any heart 
or
breath sounds. Her daughter was at the bedside, and she elected
not to pursue an autopsy. The PCP was notified, and the death
certificate was signed. 
 
Medications on Admission:
The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete.
1. trospium 20 mg oral BID 

 
Discharge Medications:
N/A
 
Discharge Disposition:
Expired
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
ALS
 
Discharge Condition:
Deceased
 
Discharge Instructions:
N/A
 
Followup Instructions:
___

and a sterile dressing applied.

complications.

Dear Ms. ___,

It was a pleasure caring for you at ___ 
___.  You were admitted to the hospital after a 
fall and were found to have a right upper arm fracture, two left 
rib fractures and a urinary tract infection.  The Orthopaedic 
Service evaluated your arm fracture and no surgery was 
necessary.  Please remain non-weight bearing on your right arm 
and continue to wear your sling for comfort.  Your rib fractures 
will heal on their own, but it is important to use your 
incentive spirometer to help keep your lungs inflated to prevent 
pneumonia.  You were started on an oral antibiotic to treat your 
urinary tract infection. 

You have worked with Physical Therapy and you are now ready to 
be discharged from the hospital.  Please follow the discharge 
instructions below to ensure a safe recovery:

Instructions for Rib Fractures: 

* Your injury caused left-sided rib fractures which can cause 
severe pain and subsequently cause you to take shallow breaths 
because of the pain.
 
* You should take your pain medication as directed to stay ahead 
of the pain otherwise you won't be able to take deep breaths. If 
the pain medication is too sedating take half the dose and 
notify your physician.
 
* Pneumonia is a complication of rib fractures.  In order to 
decrease your risk you must use your incentive spirometer 4 
times every hour while awake.  This will help expand the small 
airways in your lungs and assist in coughing up secretions that 
pool in the lungs.
 
* You will be more comfortable if you use a cough pillow to hold 
against your chest and guard your rib cage while coughing and 
deep breathing.
 
* Symptomatic relief with ice packs or heating pads for short 
periods may ease the pain.
 
* Narcotic pain medication can cause constipation therefore you 
should take a stool softener twice daily and increase your fluid 
and fiber intake if possible.
 
* Do NOT smoke
 
* If your doctor allows, non-steroidal ___ drugs 
are very effective in controlling pain ( ie, Ibuprofen, Motrin, 
Advil, Aleve, Naprosyn) but they have their own set of side 
effects so make sure your doctor approves.
 
* Return to the Emergency Room right away for any acute 
shortness of breath, increased pain or crackling sensation 
around your ribs (crepitus).

Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the 
Emergency Department for any of the following:
*You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or 
tightness.

medications.

or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid

The patient with history of chronic recurrent cholangitis was 
admitted to the General Surgical Service for elective

cholecystectomy, which went well without complication (reader

uneventful stay in the PACU, the patient arrived on the floor 
NPO, on IV fluids, with a foley catheter and a JP drain in 
place, and epidural for pain control. The patient was 
hemodynamically stable.
Post-operative pain was initially well controlled with epidural 
analgesia, which was converted to oral pain medication when 
tolerating clear liquids. The patient was started on sips of 
clears on POD# 1. Diet was progressively advanced as tolerated 
to a regular diet by POD# 2. The epidural and foley catheter was 
discontinued at midnight of POD# 4. The patient subsequently 
voided without problem. JP was discontinued on POD# 4 as the 
output was low. On POD 6, patient was noticed to have drainage

incision. On POD 7, drainage increased, patient's labs were 
noticeable for WBC of 18K. Patient's wound was opened and

sides of your groin.  You tolerated the procedure well and are

the recommendations below to ensure a speedy and uneventful 
recovery.

Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Endovascular Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Repair Discharge 
Instructions

PLEASE NOTE: After endovascular aortic repair (EVAR), it is very 
important to have regular appointments (every ___ months) for 
the rest of your life.  These appointments will include a CT 
(聯CAT聰) scan and/or ultrasound of your graft.  If you miss an 
appointment, please call to reschedule. 
WHAT TO EXPECT:
聲Bruising, tenderness, and a sensation of fullness at the groin 
puncture sites (or incisions) is normal and will go away in 
one-two weeks
CARE OF THE GROIN PUNCTURE SITES:
聲It is normal to have mild swelling, a small bruise, or small 
amounts of drainage at the groin puncture sites.  In two weeks, 
you may feel a small, painless, pea sized knot at the puncture 
sites.  This too is normal.  Male patients may notice swelling 
in the scrotum.  The swelling will get better over one-two 
weeks.
聲Look at the area daily to see if there are any changes.  Be 
sure to report signs of infection.  These include: increasing 
redness; worsening pain; new or increasing drainage, or drainage 
that is white, yellow, or green; or fever of 101.5 or more.  (If 
you have taken aspirin, Tylenol, or other fever reducing 
medicine, wait at least ___ hours after taking it before you

incision)
聲If you have sudden, severe bleeding or swelling at either of 
the groin puncture sites: 
-Lie down, keep leg straight and apply (or have someone apply) 
firm pressure to area for ___ minutes with a gauze pad or 
clean cloth. 
-Once bleeding has stopped, call your surgeon to report what 
happened.   
-If bleeding does not stop, call ___ for transfer to closest 
Emergency Room. 
聲You may shower 48 hours after surgery.  Let the soapy water 
run over the puncture sites, then rinse and pat dry.  Do not rub 
these sites and do not apply cream, lotion, ointment or powder. 
聲Wear loose-fitting pants and clothing as this will be less 
irritating to the groin puncture sites. 
MEDICATIONS
聲Take aspirin daily.  Aspirin helps prevent blood clots that 
could form in your repaired artery. 
聲It is very important that you never stop taking aspirin or 
other blood thinning medicines-even for a short while- unless 
the surgeon who repaired your aneurysm tells you it is okay to 
stop.   Do not stop taking them, even if another doctor or nurse 
tells you to, without getting an okay from the surgeon who first 
prescribed them. 
聲You will be given prescriptions for any new medication started 
during your hospital stay.
聲Before you go home, your nurse ___ give you information about 
new medication and will review all the medications you should 
take at home.  Be sure to ask any questions you may have.  If 
something you normally take or may take is not on the list you 
receive from the nurse, please ask if it is okay to take it. 
PAIN MANAGEMENT
聲Most patients do not have much pain following this procedure.  
 Your puncture sites may be a little sore.  This will improve 
daily. If it is getting worse, please let us know.
聲You will be given instructions about taking pain medicine if 
you need it.
ACTIVITY
聲You must limit activity to protect the puncture sites in your 
groin.  For ONE WEEK:
-Do not drive
-Do not swim, take a tub bath or go in a Jacuzzi or hot tub
-Do not lift, push, pull or carry anything heavier than five 
pounds
-Do not do any exercise or activity that causes you to hold your 
breath or bear down with your abdominal muscles.
-Do not resume sexual activity
聲Discuss with your surgeon when you may return to other regular 
activities, including work.  If needed, we will give you a 
letter for your workplace. 
聲It is normal to feel weak and tired.  This can last six-eight 
weeks, but should get better day by day.   You may want to have 
help around the house during this time.
___ push yourself too hard during your recovery.  Rest when 
you feel tired.  Gradually return to normal activities over the 
next month.
聲We encourage you to walk regularly. Walking, especially 
outdoors in good weather is the best exercise for circulation.  
Walk short distances at first, even in the house, then do a 
little more each day.
聲It is okay to climb stairs.  You may need to climb them slowly

DIET
聲It is normal to have a decreased appetite.  Your appetite will 
return over time.
聲Follow a well balance, heart-healthy diet, with moderate 
restriction of salt and fat. 
聲Eat small, frequent meals with nutritious food options (high 
fiber, lean meats, fruits, and vegetables) to maintain your 
strength and to help with wound healing.
BOWEL AND BLADDER FUNCTION
聲You should be able to pass urine without difficulty.  Call you

Discharge Instructions:
Please follow the recommendations below to ensure a speedy and 
uneventful recovery.
 
ACTIVITY:
- Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
- You may climb stairs.
- You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit.
- Don't lift more than 10 lbs for 6 weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
- You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
- You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
- You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. 
You might feel somewhat depressed.
- You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
- All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
- Your incision may be slightly red around the edges. This is 
normal.
- If you have steri strips, do not remove them for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that are on your incision.) But 
if they fall off before that that's okay).
- You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
- It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This 
will go away.
- Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
- Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
- You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid 
staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, 
please call your surgeon.
- You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
- Over the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
- Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.
- After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get 
diarrhea, don't take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of 
fluitds and see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is 
severe and you feel ill, please call your surgeon.
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness".
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
-You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine 
as directed.
- Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take 
more medicine at one time than prescribed.
- Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
- Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
- If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
- Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you 
cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon.

OPERATORS:  Dr. ___, radiology trainee and Dr. ___

components of the procedure and reviewed and agrees with the trainee's
findings.

TECHNIQUE:  The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were
explained to the patient.  After a detailed discussion, informed written
consent was obtained.  A pre-procedure timeout using three patient identifiers
was performed per ___ protocol.

The patient was placed in a supine position on the CT scan table. Limited
preprocedure CT scan was performed to localize the collection.  Based on the
CT findings an appropriate skin entry site for the drain placement was chosen.
The site was marked.  Local anesthesia was administered with 1% Lidocaine
solution.

Using intermittent CT fluoroscopic guidance, an 18-G ___ needle was
inserted into the collection.  A sample of fluid was aspirated, confirming
needle position within the collection.  0.038 ___ wire was placed through
the needle and needle was removed. This was followed by placement of ___
Exodus pigtail catheter into the collection. The plastic stiffener and the
wire were removed.  The pigtail was deployed. The position of the pigtail was
confirmed within the collection via CT fluoroscopy.

Approximately 40 cc of purulent fluid was aspirated with a sample sent for
microbiology evaluation. The catheter was secured by a StatLock. The catheter
was attached to suction bulb. Sterile dressing was applied.

The procedure was tolerated well, and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

[2148-01-17 19:31:00] RR: EXAMINATION:  PELVIS MRI; SECOND OPINION REQUEST

INDICATION:  Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a h/o mild dementia, renal
cell mass, HTN, presented to OSH ___ after fall found to have sigmoid
diverticulitis with intense inflammatory process involving mesentery and
uterus with associated 4.4cm intrauterine abscess.// Imaging from OSH.  MRI
imaging hazy difficult to interpret.  Hoping to avoid additional pelvic
ultrasound.  Possible underlying malignancy commented on outside read.
Wondering if possible to better characterize uterus/ abscess.

TECHNIQUE:  T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the pelvis were acquired
prior to and after the administration of intravenous contrast.  A second
opinion is requested.

COMPARISON:  None available

FINDINGS: 

The uterus is anteverted and measures 10.5 x 5.7 x 7.6 cm.  Endometrium is not
thickened.  There are multiple T2 hypointense myometrial lesions measuring up
to 2.2 cm, consistent with fibroids.  Most demonstrate mild or no significant
internal enhancement.

Evaluation of the rectum and intrapelvic bowel is notable for multiple sigmoid
diverticula.  There are extensive inflammatory changes surrounding some of the
diverticula along the sigmoid mesocolon and pericolonic fat (04:14),
consistent with acute diverticulitis.  The inflammatory changes track along
and towards the left posterior uterus, where there are small pockets of T2
hyperintense fluid demonstrating restricted diffusion, consistent with
phlegmon/abscesses (8:37, 4:14).  Within the left uterine body towards the
fundus, there is a 4.9 x 4.0 cm fluid collection, consistent with an
intrauterine abscess (04:17).  Inflammatory changes also extend towards and
involve the left ovary.  Wall thickening and perirectal fat stranding is
consistent with proctitis, likely reactive.

Bilateral ovarian cysts measure up to 2.6 cm on the right, and 1.4 cm on the
left.  The bladder is within normal limits.

There is no pelvic adenopathy.  Iliac vessels are patent.

No focal osseous lesions are identified.  Evaluation of the soft tissues is
notable for several ventral paramidline abdominal wall hernias.

IMPRESSION:


1. Findings consistent with acute diverticulitis complicated by extensive
surrounding phlegmon in the sigmoid mesocolon with inflammatory changes
extending towards the left uterus and ovary, resulting in a 4.9 cm
intrauterine abscess.
2. Likely reactive proctitis.
3. Bilateral simple appearing ovarian cysts, 2.6 cm on the right, and 1.4 cm
on the left.
--- NEXT REPORT ---
[2148-01-20 11:50:00] RR: EXAMINATION:  CT ABD AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with active diverticulitis with phlegmon
extending into L uterus/ovary w/ 4.9 cm L uterine abscess s/p ___ drain
placement// Is abscess being adequately managed by current drain/do we have
source control or is abscess enlarging?

TECHNIQUE:  Single phase contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired through the
abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration.
Oral contrast was administered.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 3.0 s, 40.2 cm; CTDIvol = 9.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 385.4
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.7
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 1.2 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 6.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 3.3
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 390 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  ___ and radionuclide renogram ___

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Visualized lung fields are within normal limits.  There is no
evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: There are small cysts in the right and caudate lobes of the
liver.  There is a 3 mm hypodensity in the left lobe of the liver that likely
represents a cyst but is too small to accurately characterize.  The liver
demonstrates otherwise homogenous attenuation throughout.  There is no
evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  There is a
heterogeneous, rounded, 14 mm structure along the anterior wall of the
gallbladder likely representing a gallstone.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: There are small cysts in the right kidney and lower pole of the left
kidney.  There also subcentimeter hypodensities in each kidney consistent with
cysts but too small to characterize.  In addition, there is a complex,,
heterogeneous, largely exophytic 4.2 x 3.5 x 4.3 cm lesion in the interpolar
right kidney that contains coarse calcification at its periphery.  Cortical
scarring is noted in the left kidney.  The kidneys are of normal and symmetric
size with normal nephrogram.  There is no hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: A moderate axial hiatal hernia is present.  Small bowel
loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.
There are multiple sigmoid diverticula.  Hazy density in the perirectal fat as
improved.  The appendix is not identified.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: There is an enlarged fibroid uterus containing multiple
coarse calcifications.  A pigtail catheter has been inserted, terminating in
the region of the previously demonstrated abscess cavity extending from the
uterine fundus into the left pelvic adnexa.  There is no apparent residual
fluid collection in this location.  There are also bilateral pelvic adnexal
cysts, the larger measuring 2.6 cm on the right, unchanged.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Extensive atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES: There are degenerative changes with disc space narrowing in the spine. 
Degenerative changes are present in the hips as well.  There is a hemangioma
of bone in the L3 vertebral body.  There is no concerning osteolytic or
osteoblastic lesion.  There is grade 1 anterior spondylolisthesis L5 on S1.

SOFT TISSUES: A moderate-size, fat containing, periumbilical hernia is
re-demonstrated.

IMPRESSION:


1. Satisfactory drainage of pelvic abscess.
2. There are persistent bilateral necks all cysts that likely represent
ovarian cysts.
3. Enlarged fibroid uterus.
4. 4.2 cm complex right renal mass consistent with neoplastic disease, which
is been reported previously.  Has this been characterized prior outside
examinations?
5. Hepatic and renal cysts.  Also subcentimeter hypodensities in the liver and
kidneys that likely represent cysts but are too small to accurately
characterize.
6. Filling defect in the gallbladder that likely represents a gallstone, but
is nonspecific in character.  This could be further characterized by
sonography.  Has this been evaluated on prior outside studies?
7. Colonic diverticulosis.
8. Hiatal hernia.
9.  Interval improvement in findings consistent with proctitis.
10. Degenerative change in the spine with grade 1 anterior spondylolisthesis
of L5 on S1.  Hemangioma of bone L3.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ yo female with history of dementia who reportedly had fever 
and altered mental status at her nursing home found to have pan 
sensitive E. Coli septicemia likely from a biliary source. 

# E. Coli Septicemia likely secondary to Cholangitis: Patient 
noted on initial blood cultures to have pan-sensitive E coli. 
She was initially started on Unasyn to cover likely biliary 
source, transitioned to ceftriaxone and then PO cefpodoxime with 
plan for total 14 day course (on day 5 of 14 on day of 
discharge). Likely biliary source of bacteria; initial concern 
for urinary source given GNR in urine, but later grew out 
pan-sensitive Klebsiella in urine. Most likely source of 
septicemia is cholangitis given initial intrahepatic ductal 
dilitation and CBD dilitation on CT; resolved by day prior to 
discharge per RUQ ultrasound. No evidence of stones in the 
gallbladder on day prior to discharge; no evidence of 
obstructive mass on imaging, and given the resolution of the 
symptoms, she likely had a transient obstruction from a stone 
that has since passed. The patient improved clinically and her 
LFTs and white count trended down, with WBC 12 on day of 
discharge.
.
# Pancreatitis: Patient with abdominal pain, elevated lipase, 
and dusky stranding around pancreas on CT scan on admission. 
Bisap score was 3 given age, SIRS criteria (fever and 
leukocytosis), and pleural effusion.  She was mildly 
hemoconcentrated with elevated hct and dry on exam.  Most likely 
cause of pancreatitis is gallstones given prominent CBD 
(although size normal for age) and intrahepatic ductal 
dilatation, though CBD diameter stable from previous imaging and 
resolved on RUQ u/s on day prior to discharge. No clear inciting 
medications, normal ___, and no EtOH. No evidence of malignancy 
causing obstruction based on CT scan, and symptoms resolved 
without intervention making persistant obstruction unlikely.  
Considered ERCP but felt risk outweighed potential benefit given 
rapid improvement with antibiotics and conservative management.  
She was initially managed conservatively with NPO, IVF, and diet 
was advanced which she tolerated. On day of discharge, abdominal 
pain resolved and all labs trending towards normal.
.
# HTN: The patient's SBP was quite labile, ranging from 100 on 
admission to 190 on the floor. Had been on metoprolol succinate 
12.5 mg daily which was held in the ICU given septicemia. 
Restarted on metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily on discharge. 
.
# Bacteruria: Patient's urine grew pan-sensitive Klebsiella. No 
urinary symptoms. Likely asymptomatic bacturia, not source of 
septicemia; covered by cefpodoxime.
.
# Umbilical hernia: Mildly tender but reducible. No signs of 
obstruction, strangulation, or incarceration. Initially elevated 
lactate, now trended down and within normal limits on discharge.
.
# Dementia: Granddaughters report she is at her baseline. 
Initially held donepizil, then later restarted.
.
# Sjogrens: Stable. Continued artificial tears.
.
# Code status: Per extensive discussion with granddaughter, 
patient now DNR/DNI.

Discharge Disposition:

Discharge Diagnosis:

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital with right lower quadrant 
pain.  You underwent a cat scan which showed acute appendicitis. 
 You were taken to the operating room to have your appendix 
removed.  You are slowly recovering from your surgery and you 
are cleared for discharge home with the following instructions:  


You were admitted to the hospital with acute appendicitis. You 
were taken to the operating room and had your appendix removed 
laparoscopically.  You tolerated the procedure well and are now 
being discharged home with the following instructions:
Please follow up at the appointment in clinic listed below. We 
also generally recommend that patients follow up with their 
primary care provider after having surgery. We have scheduled an 
appointment for you listed below.
ACTIVITY:
 
Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
You may climb stairs. 
You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until you 
see your surgeon at your next visit.
Don't lift more than ___ lbs for ___ weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
 
HOW YOU MAY FEEL: 
You may feel weak or "washed out" a couple weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
You could have a poor appetite for a couple days. Food may seem 
unappealing.
All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time.  If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
Tomorrow you may shower and remove the gauzes over your 
incisions. Under these dressings you have small plastic bandages 
called steristrips. Do not remove steri-strips for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that might be on your 
incision.) But if they fall off before that that's okay.
Your incisions may be slightly red around the stitches. This is 
normal.
You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining 
your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call 
your surgeon.
You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
Constipation is a common side effect of narcotic pain 
medicaitons. If needed, you may take a stool softener (such as 
Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk of 
magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving 
the bowels, call your surgeon. 
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness". 
Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is 
getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
You will receive a prescription for pain medicine to take by 
mouth. It is important to take this medicine as directed. Do not 
take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more 
medicine at one time than prescribed. Do not drink alcohol or 
drive while taking narcotic pain medication.
Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you cough 
or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as 
you did before, unless you have been told differently.
If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to 
take, please call your surgeon.
 
DANGER SIGNS:
Please call your surgeon if you develop:
- worsening abdominal pain
- sharp or severe pain that lasts several hours
- temperature of 101 degrees or higher
- severe diarrhea
- vomiting
- redness around the incision that is spreading
- increased swelling around the incision
- excessive bruising around the incision
- cloudy fluid coming from the wound
- bright red blood or foul smelling discharge coming from the 
wound
- an increase in drainage from the wound

in origin.

fear of eating as a result of her diarrhea, and her weight loss 
may be related to poor PO intake.  However, she did eat multiple 
meals without difficulty in the hospital.  Her weight should 
continue to be followed as an outpatient, and if weight loss 
continues, additional evaluation may be warranted.

anesthetic effect.  Under continuous ultrasound guidance, an 18 gauge core
biopsy device with a 22 mm throw was used to obtain 4 core biopsy specimens
according to lung cancer protocol, which were sent for pathology.

The procedure was tolerated well and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

SEDATION:  Moderate sedation was not administered.

FINDINGS:


1. Hypoechoic vascular mass within the right chest wall measuring 1.9 x 3.1 x
4.8 cm.
2. Right-sided pleural effusion, partially imaged.

IMPRESSION: 

Technically successful ultrasound-guided biopsy of right-sided chest wall
mass.  4 specimens (22 mm 18 gauge cores) were submitted to pathology in 2
separate formalin containers.  No immediate postprocedure complication.
